title: Salar de Uyuni Drone Shot | Epic Nature & Wildlife Short Video
creator: THE NATURE PROJECT
notes: SALT-FLAT / SALAR LANDSCAPE EXEMPLAR (subgenre: salt flat / salar landscapes) -- added following the same per-record verification rigor as this pack's original 10 entries (see e.g. bbc-earth-aerial.json, the-nature-project-horseshoe-bend.json). Independently re-verified this session (not merely trusted from the prior-agent candidate JSON) via YouTube's oEmbed endpoint (https://www.youtube.com/oembed?url=https://www.youtube.com/shorts/A53qUXVIrBo&format=json), which returned title="Salar de Uyuni Drone Shot | Epic Nature & Wildlife Short Video" and author_name="THE NATURE PROJECT" (author_url https://www.youtube.com/@THENATUREPROJECTFILMS) -- an exact match to both fields in the candidate record, confirming this source_url in this session rather than trusting the prior pass verbatim. This is the same channel as this pack's existing the-nature-project-horseshoe-bend.json exemplar, but a distinct video (Salar de Uyuni, Bolivia, salt-flat subject vs. the Horseshoe Bend desert-canyon meander), so no filename or content collision with that existing record. This is a native YouTube Shorts upload (/shorts/ URL path, oEmbed player aspect ratio 113x200 i.e. vertical) of a single aerial shot over the Salar de Uyuni salt flat, fitting the salt-flat/salar landscapes subgenre. `license_category` is set to the conservative default 'all-rights-reserved': this session fetched the raw watch-page HTML for this URL directly (curl with a desktop User-Agent) and got a 200 response (~1.6MB, containing a populated `ytInitialData` blob and one match for 'Salar' in-page -- i.e. this was the genuine watch page, not a bot-check/consent wall, unlike the prior agent's report of a blocked fetch) and grepped it case-insensitively for 'creative commons' and 'licensed under', finding zero matches for either -- i.e. no explicit CC-license marking was found on this specific upload, so per this project's stated default (assume all-rights-reserved unless the uploader's license setting is directly confirmed otherwise), `local_media_path` stays null and no video file was downloaded or persisted anywhere in this repo. `duration` is left null: YouTube oEmbed does not return a duration field for this URL and no YouTube Data API call was made this session -- rather than infer a number from the Shorts sub-60s convention, this field is left unset pending a real API call or manual playback confirmation (see reference_pack/README.md refresh/expiry process). `award_or_showcase_provenance` is left null: this is ordinary recurring channel content (Shorts-format nature/travel drone clips, same channel/genre as the existing Horseshoe Bend exemplar), not a documented award, festival selection, or press-featured showcase -- inventing a provenance string here would violate the no-placeholders constitution rule. `scores` (sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness) are `scores_provenance: manually_estimated` placeholder judgments, calibrated against this pack's existing 0.70-0.88 range and specifically anchored to this same channel's prior the-nature-project-horseshoe-bend.json baseline (sharpness=0.74, exposure=0.72, motion_smoothness=0.76): sharpness=0.75 (marginally above that baseline -- a salt flat's flat, high-texture-contrast crust plausibly renders as crisp in a well-exposed still/near-still frame, but as with the Horseshoe Bend record there is no stated drone model, output resolution, or subscriber/reach signal to justify scoring meaningfully higher); exposure=0.73 (marginally above the 0.72 baseline but still capped below this pack's high end -- the Salar de Uyuni's wet-season mirror-reflection surface, visible in the oEmbed thumbnail hq2.jpg, is a genuinely harder dynamic-range case than flat daylight terrain, being extremely bright and reflective and prone to highlight clipping, with no HDR/log-profile grading claim found anywhere to offset that risk); motion_smoothness=0.80 (above the 0.76 baseline -- salar terrain is famously flat and obstacle-free, which favours a long, steady push/reveal drone shot with none of the terrain-following corrections a canyon/cliff shot like Horseshoe Bend would need, so a smoother apparent flight path is a reasonable inference from subject geography alone, though still not a verified stabilization-mode claim). These are NOT measurements from Capability 1's actual scoring functions (scoring_sharpness.py/scoring_exposure.py/scoring_motion_smoothness.py) -- no source file was ever downloaded to run those functions against, per the no-bulk-download constraint. `composite_score` IS a real computation: it was produced this session by calling this repo's own `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.composite.compute_composite_score` with these three manually-estimated inputs (sharpness=0.75, exposure=0.73, motion_smoothness=0.80, composition=None) and `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.weights.default_weights()` via `.venv/bin/python3` in this session. `default_weights()` with no arguments returned weights_version='default-v2' (sharpness=exposure=motion_smoothness=composition=0.25 each); with composition=None, `compute_composite_score` skips the zero-information composition slot and renormalizes over only the three populated signals, so the result is the plain arithmetic mean of sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness regardless of v1-vs-v2 weighting. The call returned composite_score=0.7600000000000001, matching (0.75+0.73+0.80)/3 exactly -- rounded to 0.76 here -- so the composite arithmetic itself is genuine and reproducible, even though its inputs are estimates rather than measurements. `editorial_style`: an earlier stage of this task's research chain attempted a genuine live-playback review of this exact URL via Playwright browser automation (the claude-in-chrome extension was not connected in that environment). It confirmed the page loaded the correct video (page title/URL matched "Salar de Uyuni Drone Shot | Epic Nature & Wildlife Short Video") and that the <video> element was vertical (720x1280, consistent with Shorts format), but every attempt to pause/seek and screenshot a specific frame failed: the YouTube Shorts feed auto-advanced to a different, unrelated short (observed 3 separate times across re-navigations) faster than the tool round-trip, and two duration reads of the nominal target (30.221s vs 46.601s) disagreed, indicating the querySelector('video') calls sometimes hit a different short's video element in the feed's DOM rather than the on-screen target. No stable, verifiably-on-target frame or timestamp was ever captured, so no genuine shot/cut/transition observation could be made. A text-provenance fallback (WebFetch of the URL, WebSearch for the exact title plus creator name) surfaced only YouTube's footer/legal boilerplate and unrelated third-party Salar de Uyuni drone content (stock libraries, a different long-form Nature Project video, other creators' videos) -- no creator commentary, press coverage, or description text discussing this short's edit pacing, cut count, or transition style. Consistent with this pack's convention of not inferring `format` from URL/platform alone without an actual on-target observation (see lido-wave-nazare-coastal.json's identical reasoning for its Instagram Reel), `format` is left null here despite the /shorts/ URL strongly implying short-form -- `review_method` is therefore 'not_reviewed' and all quantitative editorial fields stay null/empty rather than guessed.

editorial_style.pacing_notes: An earlier stage of this task's research chain attempted a genuine live-playback review via Playwright browser automation (claude-in-chrome extension not connected). It confirmed the correct video loaded (title/URL match, vertical 720x1280 <video> element) but every pause/seek+screenshot attempt failed because the YouTube Shorts feed auto-advanced to a different, unrelated short faster than the tool round-trip (observed 3 times across re-navigations); two duration reads of the nominal target (30.221s vs 46.601s) disagreed, indicating querySelector('video') sometimes targeted a different short's DOM element. No stable, verifiably-on-target frame or timestamp was captured, so no genuine shot/cut observation could be made. Fell back to text-provenance: WebFetch of the URL returned only YouTube's footer/legal boilerplate, and WebSearch for the exact title plus creator name surfaced only this video's own listing and unrelated third-party Salar de Uyuni drone footage (stock libraries, a different long-form Nature Project video, other creators' videos) -- no creator commentary, press coverage, or description text discussing this short's edit pacing, cut count, or transition style was found. Leaving format/cut-count/shot-length/transitions unpopulated rather than guessing.
